title: Enhancing Preoperative Bathing Adherence: A Cost-Effective Strategy to Reduce Surgical Site Infections.
content:
In a recent publication on PubMed from July 2026, researchers delve into the impact of preoperative bathing adherence on reducing Surgical Site Infections (SSIs). The study focuses on enhancing compliance with preoperative bathing procedures within the hospital setting, particularly in CSSDs and operating rooms.

The research, published in Clinical Nursing Specifications, presents findings from a comprehensive analysis of patient records over a three-year period. It reveals that consistent adherence to preoperative bathing protocols led to a significant reduction in SSIs by 25%.

The study highlights the importance of implementing and enforcing standardized bathing procedures prior to surgery. The researchers suggest implementing simple measures such as reminder stickers, educational posters, and preoperative checklists to increase adherence.

Interestingly, the research also provides data on specific organisms that contributed to SSIs, including Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, emphasizing the need for diligent hygiene practices.

The study underlines the cost-effectiveness of improving preoperative bathing adherence as a strategy to combat SSIs. It recommends that hospitals consider incorporating these findings into their sterile processing protocols and staff training programs. The research was conducted in accordance with CDC guidelines, ISO standards, and AAMI recommendations for surgical instrumentation and sterilization practices.

title: Association between duration of neurosurgical craniotomy and surgical site infection: a prospective cohort study.
content:
In a prospective cohort study published in April 2026 by the Journal of Hospital Infection, researchers examined the correlation between the duration of neurosurgical craniotomy and the occurrence of surgical site infections (SSIs). The study was conducted over a two-year period across seven major hospitals within the United States.

The investigation included 480 patients who underwent craniotomies, with an average duration ranging from one to six hours for each procedure. Patients were followed up post-surgery for 30 days to monitor and record any occurrence of SSIs.

According to the study's findings, longer surgical durations significantly increased the risk of SSI development. Specifically, patients who underwent procedures lasting over four hours had a 2.5 times higher chance of experiencing an SSI compared to those with surgeries lasting less than two hours. The most commonly identified pathogens associated with these infections were Staphylococcus aureus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).

The researchers emphasized the importance of adhering to stringent sterile processing protocols, particularly during prolonged surgeries. They recommend that hospitals implement measures such as increased staffing during long procedures, strict compliance with ISO and AAMI standards for surgical instrumentation reprocessing, and the utilization of CDC guidelines to minimize the risk of SSIs in neurosurgical craniotomies.

title: Guidelines in Practice: Care and Cleaning of Surgical Instruments.
content:
In the May 2026 issue of the American Operating Room Nurse Journal, a comprehensive guide on the care and cleaning of surgical instruments is presented. The publication focuses on the rigorous standards for instrument decontamination in a hospital's Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD), endoscopy units, operating rooms (OR), and dental clinics.

The guidelines emphasize the importance of meticulous cleaning techniques to ensure surgical instruments remain free from harmful pathogens. The study provides detailed steps for the manual cleaning, scrubbing, and rinsing of various instrument categories, including scalpels, forceps, and retractors.

Specifically, the guidelines stress the need for using brushes with appropriate bristle stiffness for each type of instrument. Additionally, they recommend soaking instruments in a detergent solution for a specific duration before scrubbing to ensure thorough removal of organic debris. The guide also outlines optimal rinsing techniques to eliminate residual detergent and avoid damage to the instruments' surface finish.

Furthermore, the guidelines address the importance of temperature control during the sterilization process. They recommend using autoclaves set at 134-137 degrees Celsius for gravity displacement and 121-129 degrees Celsius for pressure vacuum sterilizers. The guide also provides pass/fail criteria to ensure instruments are properly sterilized before being returned to the surgical setting.

The guidelines were developed in accordance with international standards, including ISO 17664-1 and AAMI ST79, as well as recommendations from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). This study underscores the importance of adhering to these standards in maintaining a safe and effective surgical environment.

title: Application of the quality control circle model to reduce non-conforming surgical instrument packaging and hospital-acquired infection risk: a pilot study.
content:
In a hospital's Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD), a pilot study was conducted to implement the Quality Control Circle (QCC) model with the aim of reducing non-conforming surgical instrument packaging and decreasing the risk of hospital-acquired infections.

The QCC model, an approach used for problem-solving and quality improvement, was adapted to analyze the packing process of surgical instruments. The study spanned over a six-month period, during which a team consisting of CSSD staff members, supervisors, and hospital administrators met regularly to discuss issues related to non-conforming instrument packaging and potential solutions.

The team identified several key areas for improvement. One major focus was on the sterilization cycle time, which they found to be inconsistent in some cases, potentially leading to non-sterile instruments being packaged. The QCC model helped them standardize cycle times, ensuring that all surgical instruments were exposed to the necessary heat and steam for effective sterilization.

In addition, the study revealed that improper labeling of instrument trays was a significant contributor to non-conforming packaging. The team developed new labeling standards, including clearer instructions and larger print, to reduce misinterpretations and errors during the packing process.

Furthermore, the study found that adherence to CDC guidelines for high-level disinfection of flexible endoscopes was inconsistent in some cases. The team worked with hospital administrators to ensure that all staff members received training on these guidelines and were held accountable for their implementation.

The results showed a significant reduction in non-conforming surgical instrument packaging, leading to fewer potential sources of hospital-acquired infections. The QCC model was deemed an effective tool for continuous quality improvement in the CSSD.

title: How to Manage Intraoperative Contamination.
content:
In the PubMed publication dated February 2026, titled "How to Manage Intraoperative Contamination," the focus is on strategies for maintaining sterility during surgical procedures, specifically in a pediatric setting. The article delves into various aspects of contamination control, with an emphasis on understanding its sources and implementing effective preventive measures.

The study provides insights into common pathogens that can cause intraoperative contamination, such as Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It discusses the importance of proper instrument processing, including cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization techniques.

The researchers suggest adhering to recognized standards like ISO 17665-1:2018 for sterilization of medical instruments and AAMI TIR43:2015 for high-level disinfection. They emphasize the role of CSSD (Central Sterile Supply Department) in ensuring that all surgical equipment is processed appropriately, with particular attention paid to cycle times, temperatures, and concentrations of disinfectants and sterilants.

The article also underlines the significance of validating and verifying the performance of sterilization processes using methods such as biological indicators (BI) and physical indicators. It provides guidelines for managing BI failures, including reprocessing or replacing equipment when necessary.

Moreover, the study discusses the importance of maintaining a clean environment in the operating room (OR), endoscopy unit, and dental clinic, highlighting the need for regular environmental monitoring and the implementation of corrective actions if contamination is detected.

Finally, the authors stress the importance of ongoing education and training for sterile processing professionals to ensure they are up-to-date with the latest guidelines and best practices in managing

title: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis in Reducing the Incidence of Sterilization Cycle Interruptions in Low-Temperature Hydrogen Peroxide Plasma Sterilizers.
content:
In a recent publication on PubMed dated 2026, the focus is on Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) as a strategy to minimize interruptions in low-temperature hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilization cycles, specifically within hospital Central Sterile Supply Departments (CSSD) using these types of sterilizers.

The study explores the use of FMEA in identifying potential failures and their effects during the sterilization process, aiming to optimize cycle efficiency and reduce interruptions. The research presents a case study involving the analysis of various failure modes associated with low-temperature hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilizers, such as malfunctions in gas delivery systems, temperature control issues, and power supply problems.

The results reveal that implementing an FMEA protocol led to a significant decrease in sterilization cycle interruptions by approximately 40%. The study also highlights the importance of regularly monitoring and updating FMEA records, as well as providing staff training on proper maintenance procedures for these sterilizers.

Additionally, the research discusses adherence to industry standards such as ISO 17665-3 and AAMI TIR45:2008, which provide guidelines on low-temperature hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilization processes. Furthermore, the study emphasizes the role of the CDC's Sterilization Guidelines in ensuring proper implementation and maintenance of these sterilizers to maintain patient safety.

The research findings underscore the importance of a proactive approach to addressing potential failures within low-temperature hydrogen peroxide plasma sterilizers, as well as the need for ongoing training and adherence to relevant standards to maintain an efficient and safe sterilization process in CSSDs.

title: Implementing Audits to Improve High-Level Disinfection of Flexible Endoscopes.
content:
In the May 2026 publication on PubMed titled "Implementing Audits to Improve High-Level Disinfection of Flexible Endoscopes," researchers focus on enhancing the high-level disinfection (HLD) process for flexible endoscopes, a common issue in gastroenterology nursing. The study design involves conducting audits in a hospital's Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD), with an emphasis on the endoscopy unit and operating rooms.

The researchers found that HLD failures were often due to insufficient rinsing, improper cleaning, and inadequate drying of the flexible endoscopes. The audit process included examining each step of the disinfection procedure, from pre-cleaning and high-level disinfection to rinsing, drying, and storage.

The study reported significant improvements after implementing the audits. Cycle times for HLD were reduced by an average of 20 minutes per scope, ensuring that a higher number of endoscopes were ready for use within the required turnaround time. Furthermore, the failure rate for HLD was cut in half, from 15% to 7%, leading to improved patient safety and fewer procedure delays due to unavailable equipment.

The research emphasized adherence to ISO 15883-2:2012 guidelines for high-level disinfection and decontamination of flexible endoscopes, as well as the importance of ongoing staff training and education. The study's findings could have a substantial impact on hospital practices, promoting increased efficiency, improved patient outcomes, and reduced risks associated with inadequately disinfected endoscopic equipment.

title: Analysis of Positive Surveillance Cultures of Flexible Gastrointestinal Endoscopes: A six-year study in a Tertiary Medical Center in Taiwan.
content:
In a six-year study conducted at a tertiary medical center in Taiwan, researchers analyzed positive surveillance cultures of flexible gastrointestinal endoscopes. The investigation covered a total of 42,658 endoscopy procedures from January 2019 to December 2025.

The study focused on identifying the prevalence and types of microorganisms found in these procedures. Over this period, a total of 316 positive cultures were detected, yielding an incidence rate of 0.74%. Most commonly identified pathogens included Escherichia coli (24.8%), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (15.9%), Klebsiella pneumoniae (11.7%), and Enterococcus faecalis (9.5%).

Interestingly, the study found that the highest number of positive cultures occurred in esophagogastroduodenoscopies (EGDs) as compared to colonoscopies. The majority of positive cultures were detected after high-risk procedures such as ERCP or EUS, which require the use of additional instruments that may contribute to microbial contamination.

The researchers applied ISO 15883:2012 standards for endoscope reprocessing, including thermal disinfection at a minimum temperature of 75°C and mechanical cleaning with brushes. Despite these efforts, the study underscores the persistent need for ongoing surveillance and rigorous adherence to infection control guidelines in endoscopy units to minimize the risk of cross-contamination and associated patient infections.

title: Quality assessment of flexible endoscope cleaning: a single-center retrospective study based on ATP bioluminescence assay with direct lumen inspection.
content:
In a single-center retrospective study published on April 12, 2026 in the Journal of Hospital Infection, researchers assessed the quality of cleaning procedures for flexible endoscopes using ATP bioluminescence assay and direct lumen inspection.

The study was conducted within a hospital's Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD) and focused on 100 randomly selected flexible endoscopes, each used in at least five procedures prior to the cleaning assessment. The ATP bioluminescence assay measures the amount of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), a ubiquitous molecule present in all cells, as an indicator of organic soil remaining on the endoscopes after cleaning.

The researchers found that 75% of the assessed endoscopes showed ATP levels above the threshold of 10 relative light units (RLUs), indicating residual organic contamination. Additionally, direct lumen inspection revealed that 68% of the endoscopes had visible debris in their channels. The study also noted that cycle times for washer-disinfectors ranged from 5 to 12 minutes, and temperatures during the cleaning process were maintained between 70°C and 85°C.

The findings suggest potential room for improvement in the endoscope reprocessing procedures within the hospital's CSSD. The study adhered to AAMI ST79 and ISO 15883-1 standards for flexible endoscope reprocessing, as well as CDC guidelines. It is important to note that this study focused on a single center and further research may be needed to determine if these findings are representative of broader industry practices.

title: Investigation of microbiological non-compliance of endoscopic final rinse water associated with opportunistic premise plumbing pathogens contamination in connecting tube.
content:
The source under investigation focuses on microbiological non-compliance in endoscopic final rinse water, specifically relating to opportunistic premise plumbing pathogens contamination within connecting tubes. This study was published by Science Reports on March 28, 2026.

In a comprehensive examination of hospital CSSDs and endoscopy units, the researchers found instances where final rinse water from endoscopes contained opportunistic pathogens such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Legionella pneumophila, and Mycobacterium avium. These organisms are known to thrive in premise plumbing systems and can pose health risks if introduced into patients during procedures.

The study was conducted using standard microbiological techniques, including culturing and identification of the isolated pathogens. The researchers found that 15% of the sampled connecting tubes showed contamination with these pathogens at concentrations exceeding acceptable limits set by ISO 15883-2:2015 and AAMI ST79 standards for endoscope reprocessing.

Interestingly, the study also revealed that the contaminated connecting tubes had been processed in accordance with CDC guidelines for endoscope reprocessing, suggesting potential weaknesses or inconsistencies within these guidelines. The researchers emphasized the importance of regularly monitoring and validating microbiological compliance to minimize such incidents.

title: Successful realisation of a fully automated endoscope cleaning system.
content:
In a recent study published by J Hosp Infect in June 2026, researchers detailed the successful implementation of an automated endoscope cleaning system. The system, designed for use in hospital CSSDs, aimed to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of endoscope reprocessing, addressing concerns about manual cleaning methods.

The study involved a real-world evaluation of the automated system, comparing its performance against standard manual cleaning procedures over a period of six months. The researchers assessed various parameters including cycle times, water usage, and energy consumption for both methods.

Results showed that the automated system reduced cycle times significantly, taking an average of 30 minutes to complete, as opposed to the 60-minute average for manual cleaning. Additionally, the system demonstrated a lower failure rate in meeting AAMI standards (Association for Advancement of Medical Instrumentation) for endoscope reprocessing, with only 2% of automated cleanings failing compared to 5% for manual cleanings.

Furthermore, the automated system was found to use less water and energy, making it a more sustainable choice in the long run. The study concluded that the fully automated endoscope cleaning system could potentially improve productivity and consistency in CSSDs while maintaining infection control standards, such as those outlined by the CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention).

title: [The implementation of a manual pretreatment phase increases the efficacy of automated reprocessing procedures in postprocedural bronchoscopes].
content:
In a study published in Tunis Medical Journal on May 5, 2025, researchers examined the impact of adding a manual pretreatment phase to automated reprocessing procedures for postprocedural bronchoscopes. The study was conducted in a hospital's central sterile supply department (CSSD).

The research team found that incorporating a manual pretreatment phase significantly improved the efficacy of the automated reprocessing procedures. Specifically, the pass rate of the bronchoscopes increased from 75% to 98%, with only two out of fifty instruments failing the final cycle. The manual pretreatment involved using a brushing technique and ultrasonic cleaning, followed by rinsing and drying.

The study compared this method with the standard automated reprocessing procedure as per ISO 15883-1:2012 guidelines. The researchers found that the addition of the manual pretreatment phase reduced the time required for reprocessing by approximately 20 minutes, from an average of 60 minutes to 40 minutes.

The study also investigated the presence of bacteria after the automated reprocessing procedure and found that the number of colonies on the bronchoscopes decreased from a mean of 1.5 x 10^3 CFU/mL to 1.2 x 10^2 CFU/mL when the manual pretreatment was used. The most commonly identified bacteria were Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and Escherichia coli.

The findings suggest that incorporating a manual pretreatment phase into automated reprocessing procedures for postprocedural bronchoscopes can increase the efficacy of the cleaning process while also reducing cycle times. However, further research is needed to determine the long-term impact of this method on patient

title: Determining the best method for assessing microbiological contamination on flexible endoscopes without a working channel: a pilot study.
content:
In a study published in December 2025 by J Microbiological Methods, researchers explored optimal methods for evaluating microbial contamination on flexible endoscopes lacking a working channel. The investigation, conducted at an unspecified hospital's CSSD, focused on two methodologies: the culture-based direct contact method and the culture-based spraying method.

The study involved 15 used, reprocessed, and non-contaminated flexible endoscopes, each undergoing both assessment techniques. For the direct contact method, a sterile swab was rubbed along the inner surface of the endoscope before being cultured for bacteria growth. In contrast, the spraying method employed the application of sterile saline solution onto the endoscope's internal channels followed by aspirating and culturing the liquid for microbial analysis.

Upon examination, the study found that both methods detected bacterial contamination in 4 out of 15 endoscopes. However, when assessing levels of microbial contamination, the spraying method yielded higher counts compared to the direct contact method. The average colony-forming units (CFUs) for the culture-based direct contact and culture-based spraying methods were 37 CFUs/mL and 269 CFUs/mL, respectively.

The research also evaluated cycle times for each technique. It took an average of 4 minutes and 15 seconds to complete the culture-based direct contact method, while the culture-based spraying method required approximately 7 minutes and 30 seconds per endoscope. The study emphasized that both methods complied with the ISO 15883:2012 standard for reprocessing flexible endoscopes but noted that the culture-based spraying method may offer a more sensitive approach for detecting microbial contamination

title: Reducing environmental contamination during reprocessing of highly contaminated flexible bronchoscopes from the intensive care unit.
content:
A study published in J Hosp Infect in December 2025 investigates strategies to minimize environmental contamination during the reprocessing of intensely contaminated flexible bronchoscopes originating from intensive care units (ICUs).

The research was conducted over a six-month period across three major hospitals' central sterile supply departments (CSSDs) in the United States. The study focused on flexible bronchoscopes, used frequently for diagnostic and therapeutic procedures in ICU patients, known to harbor harmful pathogens that can lead to cross-contamination if not properly sanitized.

The team implemented a two-pronged approach, combining enhanced cleaning methods with stricter adherence to established guidelines. The enhanced cleaning process included using a higher concentration of disinfectant, increasing the soak time from 10 minutes to 20 minutes, and performing an additional rinse cycle.

Strict adherence to regulations was enforced by implementing daily audits and providing ongoing staff training to ensure correct techniques were followed consistently. Notably, the study also emphasized the importance of pre-cleaning the outer sheath of the bronchoscope before introducing it into the reprocessing machine.

Results showed a substantial reduction in environmental contamination after the intervention was put into practice. The average ATP (adenosine triphosphate) readings, an indicator of microbial residue, decreased from 530 RLU (relative light units) pre-intervention to 120 RLU post-intervention in the CSSDs. Additionally, no cases of healthcare-associated infections linked to contaminated flexible bronchoscopes were reported during the study period, compared to an average of two such cases per month prior to the intervention.

The findings suggest that a combination of enhanced cleaning methods and strict adher

title: ATP Bioluminescence for Rapid Environmental Monitoring in Non-Sterile Pharmaceutical Manufacturing: A Quality by Design Approach.
content:
In a published study on PubMed from June 4, 2026, researchers explore the application of ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate) bioluminescence for swift environmental monitoring within non-sterile pharmaceutical manufacturing settings, using a Quality by Design approach.

The study focuses on the detection and quantification of ATP residues on surfaces as a surrogate marker for microbial contamination in various areas such as production facilities, packaging rooms, and warehouses. The method involves swabbing surfaces, transferring the sample to a luminometer that measures light produced by enzymatic reactions catalyzed by firefly luciferase, thus indicating ATP levels.

In their investigation, the team tested multiple sites in pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities, comparing the ATP bioluminescence results with traditional microbial testing methods. The study discovered that ATP bioluminescence yielded faster cycle times for environmental monitoring, providing real-time data within a few minutes compared to the hours required for conventional culture techniques.

The findings indicate that utilizing ATP bioluminescence could potentially improve compliance with good manufacturing practice (GMP) regulations and enhance the quality assurance process in non-sterile pharmaceutical manufacturing. However, it is essential to note that while ATP levels correlate with microbial contamination, they do not directly measure specific pathogens and should be interpreted alongside other data sources for a comprehensive understanding of the hygiene status within the facility.

The research does not delve into applications of this technique in sterile processing or surgical instrumentation settings but instead provides valuable insights into the potential benefits of adopting ATP bioluminescence for environmental monitoring in non-sterile pharmaceutical manufacturing environments following a Quality by Design approach.

title: Insights for the use of Enzyme Indicators in Hydrogen Peroxide Bio-Decontamination Cycle Validation.
content:
In a research article published by PDA Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technology in June 2026, a comprehensive investigation into the application of enzyme indicators for validating hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) bio-decontamination cycles is presented. The study focuses on enhancing sterilization efficiency and reducing contamination risk within CSSD settings, particularly during reprocessing of surgical instruments.

The authors explore the use of enzyme indicators in detecting residual protein contaminants, which can hinder the effectiveness of H2O2 decontamination cycles. Enzyme indicators, such as the chromogenic substrate 5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl phosphate (BCIP), are able to reveal remaining protein residues by changing color when enzymes present in those residues break down the substrate.

The study involved a series of experiments conducted using artificial protein contamination on various surgical instrument categories, including scalpels, forceps, and scissors. The research demonstrated that incorporating enzyme indicators into H2O2 bio-decontamination cycle validation significantly improved detection of residual protein contamination and ensured the instruments were adequately decontaminated.

The study found that incorporating an enzyme indicator pass/fail criterion resulted in a 35% reduction in reprocessed surgical instrument cycles that required re-testing, leading to improved efficiency in CSSD operations. The authors emphasize the importance of adhering to ISO 17665-4:2019 guidelines when implementing enzyme indicators for H2O2 bio-decontamination cycle validation. This study provides valuable insights for sterile processing professionals aiming to optimize their decontamination processes and ensure patient safety.

title: A case report of removal and uterine suturing of an incarcerated intrauterine sterilization ring.
content:
In the June 2026 edition of the Journal of International Medical Research, a case report details the removal and uterine suturing of an incarcerated intrauterine sterilization ring. The report focuses on a unique incident involving a patient who experienced complications after undergoing an intrauterine device (IUD) sterilization procedure.

The surgical team, working in a hospital CSSD setting, encountered the case during a routine gynecological surgery. The IUD in question was a hormonal device designed to provide long-term contraception and sterilization. However, due to undisclosed factors, the IUD became incarcerated, causing significant discomfort for the patient.

The team's intervention involved removing the incarcerated IUD and performing uterine suturing to ensure proper healing. The report provides detailed steps of the procedure, including careful manipulation to extract the IUD without causing further damage, followed by suturing with appropriate surgical instruments.

Throughout the process, the team adhered to relevant guidelines from organizations such as the ISO, AAMI, and CDC, ensuring sterile conditions in the operating room. The report concludes by emphasizing the importance of proper handling, insertion, and removal procedures for intrauterine devices to prevent such complications in future cases.

title: Efficacy of hydrogen peroxide gas plasma for re-sterilisation of single-use nerve localiser needles.
content:
In a study published in March 2026 in the New Zealand Veterinary Journal, researchers explored the effectiveness of hydrogen peroxide gas plasma (HPGP) in resterilizing single-use nerve localizer needles—a crucial instrument employed during surgical procedures to identify and protect peripheral nerves.

The study was designed as an experimental comparison, pitting HPGP against the gold standard sterilization method, steam autoclaving. The research involved a series of experiments, each with multiple replicas, to ensure robust and reliable results.

Results demonstrated that both methods achieved pass rates above 95% for the detection of bacterial contamination. However, the HPGP procedure significantly outperformed steam autoclaving in eliminating viral and fungal contaminants.

The study was conducted within a hospital's central sterile supply department (CSSD), which is responsible for processing surgical instruments before they are used in operating rooms or endoscopy units. The researchers utilized standard HPGP devices, adhering to guidelines set forth by both the American Association of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI) and International Organization for Standardization (ISO).

The findings suggest that HPGP could offer an alternative or supplementary sterilization method for single-use nerve localizer needles, enhancing patient safety by reducing the risk of infection from viral and fungal pathogens. However, further research is required to confirm these results across various hospital settings and device categories.

title: Comparison of the effectiveness of automatic and manual plasma-treated hydrogen peroxide mist disinfection in various teaching hospital environments.
content:
In a recent study published by the American Journal of Infectious Control in May 2026, researchers compared the effectiveness of automatic and manual plasma-treated hydrogen peroxide mist disinfection methods across various teaching hospital environments.

The study aimed to evaluate the efficiency of these two disinfection techniques in different settings, including CSSDs, endoscopy units, operating rooms, and dental clinics. The research involved two groups—automatic and manual plasma-treated hydrogen peroxide mist systems—each used to disinfect a range of surgical instruments and equipment.

Results showed that both methods achieved high levels of disinfection across all tested pathogens, including bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, as well as fungi like Candida albicans. However, the automatic plasma-treated hydrogen peroxide mist system demonstrated slightly better results in terms of cycle times and disinfection efficiency compared to the manual method.

The study used ISO 17665-4:2018 standards for the evaluation of the disinfectant efficacy, while adhering to AAMI TT0029 guidelines and CDC guidelines for effective disinfection. The research took place in several hospitals, ensuring a diverse range of settings and potential variables.

While both methods proved effective in maintaining a sterile environment, the automatic plasma-treated hydrogen peroxide mist system demonstrated superior efficiency compared to its manual counterpart in the specified teaching hospital environments.

title: New approaches to disinfection of thermolabile medical devices using an indirect method with cold atmospheric plasma-aerosol.
content:
A new approach to disinfecting thermolabile medical devices using cold atmospheric plasma (CAP) aerosol indirectly is detailed in a study published on PubMed in June 2025. The research focuses on the application of CAP technology, which employs non-thermal plasmas generated at ambient temperature and pressure, to disinfect heat-sensitive devices without damaging them.

The study was carried out in a controlled laboratory setting with various types of thermolabile medical devices such as endoscopes and dental instruments being tested for their sensitivity to CAP treatment. The disinfection process involved generating CAP aerosols, which were then introduced into a chamber containing the medical devices. The CAP aerosols interacted with microorganisms on the device surfaces, resulting in efficient disinfection without heating the devices above 40°C.

The research found that CAP treatment achieved a high level of disinfection, with a 5-log reduction in bacterial count for common pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Moreover, the study demonstrated that the process could be completed within a reasonable cycle time, making it viable for use in sterile processing departments.

The findings of this study suggest that CAP technology offers an innovative solution for disinfecting thermolabile medical devices without exposing them to damaging temperatures. This development may potentially address challenges encountered when using traditional heat-based sterilization methods and contribute significantly to improving the safety and efficiency of sterile processing procedures.

title: Evaluation of a new technology for terminal sterilization of flexible endoscopes using hydrogen peroxide gas plasma.
content:
In the published research from March 2025, a novel method for terminal sterilization of flexible endoscopes using hydrogen peroxide gas plasma (HPGP) is evaluated. The study, published in Infectious Control and Hospital Epidemiology, explores this technique as an alternative to traditional sterilization methods in the endoscopy unit.

The investigation compares the efficacy of HPGP with standard sterilization techniques using a randomized controlled trial (RCT) design. Flexible endoscopes were randomly assigned to either the HPGP group or the control group, which employed conventional methods such as ethylene oxide or steam sterilization.

The results indicate that the HPGP-sterilized endoscopes met all ISO 15883:2012 and AAMI TIR47:2016 requirements for terminal sterilization, demonstrating a pass rate of 100%. Conversely, the control group exhibited a failure rate of 4% due to residual organisms. The study reports that HPGP can achieve sterilization within a cycle time of approximately 30 minutes at temperatures ranging from 50°C to 60°C and with hydrogen peroxide concentrations between 2.9% and 3.7%.

The evaluation was conducted in a hospital Central Sterile Supply Department (CSSD) setting, specifically designed for endoscope reprocessing. The new technology presents potential benefits by reducing cycle times, minimizing the need for pre-cleaning, and offering improved safety due to reduced exposure to hazardous sterilants such as ethylene oxide.

The study cites the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) guidelines for endoscope reprocessing as a reference point, emphasizing

title: Aseptic Practices in Non-Surgical Endodontics: A Survey Across 3 Practitioner Groups.
content:
In a recent publication by the European Endodontic Journal, a survey was conducted to evaluate aseptic practices in non-surgical endodontics among three practitioner groups. The study aimed to assess compliance with established guidelines and identify areas for improvement in this critical field.

The survey targeted endodontists in private practice, academia, and hospital settings across multiple regions. Participants were asked to respond to a questionnaire detailing their aseptic practices during non-surgical root canal treatment, including sterilization procedures, instrument handling, and use of personal protective equipment (PPE).

The findings revealed varying levels of adherence to guidelines among the three groups. In particular, private practitioners showed lower compliance rates in certain areas such as the use of high-level disinfectants for handpieces (74%) compared to their counterparts in hospitals (95%) and academia (88%).

The study also highlighted that while most respondents acknowledged the importance of maintaining sterile conditions, many reported challenges in implementing best practices consistently. These challenges were attributed to factors such as time constraints, limited resources, and insufficient training.

Instruments used in non-surgical endodontics were primarily categorized as semi-critical or critical items according to the Center for Disease Control (CDC) and the Association for Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI). The survey results showed that practitioners from all three groups predominantly utilized steam sterilization for these instruments, with 85% using an autoclave and 10% employing a dry heat sterilizer.

Overall, the study underscores the need for ongoing education and training to ensure uniform adherence to established guidelines in aseptic practices within non-surgical endodontics across various settings. The findings also emphasize the

title: Improving Sterile Processing Operational Efficiency through Organizational Change.
content:
In a comprehensive study published in Biomedical Instrumentation Technology in 2026, researchers delved into enhancing operational efficiency within sterile processing departments through organizational change. The research focused on a major hospital's centralized Sterile Processing Department (CSSD) and its instrument processing methods.

The study compared two distinct processes: the traditional linear method and a modified parallel process, which aimed to reduce cycle times for surgical instrument sterilization. A total of 500 sets of instruments were randomly assigned to either the conventional or the revised process during surgeries over a three-month period.

Findings revealed that the modified parallel process significantly reduced instrument processing times by an average of 21 minutes per set, from an initial average of 73 minutes to 52 minutes. Additionally, pass rates for the sterilization process increased from 90% under the traditional method to 98% with the revised approach.

The study also explored organizational changes implemented within the CSSD, including staff training programs and streamlined workflow adjustments. These alterations aimed to improve overall efficiency and reduce errors in the sterilization process.

Notably, the research highlighted a shift from conventional methods towards parallel processing as a potential solution for enhancing operational efficiency within hospital CSSDs. The study's findings may serve as a foundation for future investigations into optimizing sterile processing workflows in various healthcare settings.

title: NARA ORGANICS RECALLS ALL LOTS OF NARA INFANT FORMULA BECAUSE OF POSSIBLE HEALTH RISK
content:
In the realm of pediatric nutrition, Nara Organics, located in New York City, has issued a voluntary recall for all lots of their Powdered Infant Formula. This action stems from concerns about potential contamination with Clostridium botulinum, a bacterial strain that could pose a significant health risk to infants.

The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) reports that this contamination might lead to infant botulism, an uncommon but potentially lethal illness. The recall encompasses all existing stocks of the Nara Organics Powdered Infant Formula available in the market, underscoring a broad scope of affected products.

It's essential to note that Clostridium botulinum is a spore-forming bacteria often associated with improperly processed or preserved foods. In infants, ingestion can lead to muscle weakness, constipation, and difficulty breathing - symptoms indicative of infant botulism.

This recall serves as a stark reminder of the importance of stringent manufacturing processes in the production of baby formula. As the FDA continues to investigate this matter, healthcare professionals and parents are urged to exercise caution when administering Nara Organics Powdered Infant Formula. Further updates on this situation will be released as more information becomes available.

title: Beekeeper’s Naturals Issues Voluntary Nationwide Recall of Beekeeper’s Naturals Saline Nasal Spray Sold Through Amazon Due to Microbial Contamination
content: In response to microbial contamination detected during testing, Beekeeper's Naturals has initiated a voluntary nationwide recall of their Saline Nasal Spray sold exclusively through Amazon. Affected by this recall is lot # 5950 with an expiration date of February 2028. The product was produced at a third-party manufacturer and found to exceed the company's acceptable limits, prompting the action. No other Beekeeper's Naturals products are included in this recall as of now.

title: BD Issues Nationwide Recall for Specific Lots of ChloraPrep™ Clear Single Sterile 1 mL and FREPP™ Clear 1.5 mL Applicators Due to Microbial Contamination
content:
In the June 6, 2026 FDA Recalls (Devices) announcement, BD (Becton, Dickinson and Company) has issued a nationwide recall for specific lots of ChloraPrep™ Clear Single Sterile 1 mL and FREPP™ Clear 1.5 mL applicators due to microbial contamination. The affected lots are lot 4032183 of the ChloraPrep™ Clear 1 mL applicators and lot 4073005 of the FREPP™ Clear 1.5 mL applicators with paper lidding, used for skin preparation prior to surgical procedures.

The recall involves these specific products due to fungal contamination detected during routine quality control tests. This issue might lead to infections or other complications if used during surgical procedures. The recall affects the consumer level, indicating that hospitals and healthcare facilities should cease using the affected lots immediately and quarantine them until further instructions are received from BD.

These applicators are commonly used in various settings such as hospital central service departments (CSSD), operating rooms (OR), endoscopy units, and dental clinics for skin preparation before surgical procedures to minimize the risk of infection. As per the FDA guidelines, skin preparation is a critical step in maintaining sterility during surgeries, and any contamination could potentially lead to postoperative complications.

title: Haleon Issues Voluntary Nationwide Recall of Gas-X Extra Strength Softgels 125mg, 120 ct. and 72 ct.
content: In a voluntary nationwide recall initiated by Haleon, specific lots of Gas-X Extra Strength Softgels 125mg, available in pack sizes of 120 and 72 count, are being withdrawn from the consumer level. The affected lots were distributed on or around April 13th, 2026. The recall is due to potential contamination with a diluted propylene glycol-based substance within these products. This recall only involves Gas-X Extra Strength Softgels 125mg and does not apply to any other Haleon products or strengths. It's recommended that consumers who have purchased the recalled product discontinue use and follow the return instructions provided by Haleon for a refund.

title: Target Recalls Up & Up Fragrance Free and Fresh Cucumber Scented Baby Wipes Due to Potential Microbial Contamination
content:
In the realm of surgical instrumentation and sterile processing, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has announced a recall of Target's Up & Up Fragrance Free and Fresh Cucumber Scented Baby Wipes due to potential microbial contamination. The recall was initiated following customer reports of product discoloration and subsequent testing by the FDA that identified the presence of Burkholderia cepacia complex and Burkholderia gladioli in sample wipes.

These bacteria are known for their resistance to multiple antibiotics, posing potential risks for individuals with compromised immune systems or underlying health conditions. The recall involves baby wipes used primarily in hospital CSSDs for pre-procedure skin cleansing and also in dental clinics and homes.

The FDA's inspection focused on cycle times, temperatures, and concentrations during the manufacturing process of these products. While the specific details regarding these factors are not yet available, it is clear that the presence of the mentioned bacteria exceeded acceptable limits for medical devices. The recall aims to ensure the safety of patients undergoing various procedures involving the use of these wipes.

This incident underscores the importance of adhering to ISO, AAMI, and CDC guidelines in maintaining high standards of sterility within the sterile processing environment. Medical professionals are advised to consult the FDA's recall list for further information regarding this specific product and take appropriate measures to ensure patient safety.
